title: Highest temperature in London on July 11?
description:
This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the London City Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 11 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the London City Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/gb/london/EGLC.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest Met Office guidance points to a 30°C maximum in London on July 11 amid an established heatwave driven by a strong high-pressure system promoting clear skies, light winds, and strong insolation. Ensemble forecasts from ECMWF and GFS show modest spread around this value due to uncertainties in boundary-layer mixing and urban heat-island amplification, which can add 1–2°C in central districts. With the heatwave already delivering 32–34°C peaks earlier in the week, traders weigh the risk of slight cooling from increasing high cloud against continued southerly flow. Resolution hinges on the official Met Office daily maximum recorded at standard sites; model updates over the next 48 hours will refine the precise outcome between 28–31°C.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent model runs from Météo-France and ECMWF place Paris’s July 11 maximum between 34–36 °C amid a persistent high-pressure ridge and subsiding air that favors strong daytime heating under mostly clear skies. Low-level winds remain light, limiting mixing and allowing the urban heat-island effect to amplify readings at official stations. Ensemble spread arises mainly from minor differences in forecast cloud timing and boundary-layer moisture; a slightly earlier marine layer or isolated convective cell could cap the peak near 34 °C, while continued subsidence and drier air would push it toward 36–37 °C. With resolution only 48 hours away, traders are weighting the latest 12–18 UTC model cycles most heavily, producing the tight clustering around the 34–36 °C outcomes.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current ensemble forecasts from the Korea Meteorological Administration and global models place Seoul’s July 11 daytime high near 30–32 °C under lingering East Asian monsoon conditions. Moisture-laden southwesterly flow, variable cloud cover, and scattered showers introduce a roughly two-degree spread across runs, keeping trader probabilities tightly clustered around the 30 °C (20 %), 31 °C (24.5 %), and 32 °C (24 %) bins. A stronger ridge building from the Yellow Sea could push readings toward 33 °C or higher, while an approaching trough or heavier precipitation would favor the lower 29–30 °C outcomes. Historical early-July maxima cluster between 25–30 °C, providing climatological context but little resolution for the specific synoptic setup two days out. Updated KMA guidance and next model cycles remain the key near-term catalysts.

eventMetadata.context_description: Persistent high pressure over the Anatolian plateau and clear skies are anchoring trader consensus around a 29–31°C peak for Ankara on July 11, with 30°C holding the highest implied probability at 34.5%. Official numerical models and climatological baselines place the July daily maximum near 30–31°C under typical ridge conditions, while recent forecasts from multiple sources converge on 29–31°C amid light northerly winds and negligible cloud cover or precipitation. The spread across adjacent bins reflects residual uncertainty in afternoon mixing and exact steering, as small shifts in model guidance or boundary-layer moisture could nudge the observed high by 1–2°C. Updated short-range runs from global ensembles will refine this range ahead of the July 10–11 period.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Wellington Intl Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/nz/wellington/NZWN.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Wellington’s mid-July maximum temperatures typically average near 12°C under climatological norms, yet the market’s tight clustering around 10°C (46% implied probability) and 11°C (36.5%) reflects near-term forecast guidance favoring slightly cooler conditions. Primary drivers include strengthening southerly flow behind a passing front, which advects cooler maritime air from the Southern Ocean, combined with increased cloud cover that limits daytime heating. Model consensus from agencies such as NIWA and MetService shows daily highs for 11 July in the 9–11°C range, with modest uncertainty arising from exact timing of any northerly surge or clearing that could push readings to 12°C or hold them at 9°C. Seasonal outlooks indicate near- or below-average temperatures are equally likely through July, consistent with trader positioning that assigns low odds to warmer extremes above 12°C.

eventMetadata.context_description: Active monsoon conditions over northern India, with persistent cloud cover, humidity, and scattered rain or thundershowers, represent the dominant factor holding Lucknow's July 11 maximum near 31–34°C. Official forecasts from sources like IMD and AccuWeather project highs in this band, consistent with climatological suppression of peak temperatures during peak monsoon versus the 40°C+ extremes seen earlier in summer. Slight variations in rainfall timing, intensity, or steering patterns across models create the tight clustering of implied probabilities, as even modest differences in insolation or convective activity can shift the daily high by 1–2°C. Updated model runs and the next 48-hour observational data will likely refine these odds as the event nears.

Recent short-range forecasts show partly cloudy skies and highs of 86–88°F (30–31°C), aligning with the market’s heaviest weighting on 31°C. Resolution hinges on the exact recorded maximum at the official Tel Aviv station; any late-model shift toward stronger subsidence or reduced sea breeze could nudge probabilities toward 32–33°C before Sunday’s close.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus clusters around 31–32°C for Shanghai’s July 11 high, reflecting the latest ensemble model runs from global and regional centers that project daytime maxima in this narrow band under persistent subtropical high pressure. Early-July conditions in the Yangtze River Delta typically feature humid air masses with limited diurnal cooling, urban heat-island effects at official stations, and modest variability from any weak convective cells or steering-flow adjustments. Historical analogs and July climatology center near 31°C, while current model spread supports the tight probability distribution across 30–33°C outcomes; fresh guidance updates over the next 48 hours will narrow resolution criteria tied to the precise 24-hour maximum recorded at the reference meteorological site.

eventMetadata.context_description: Singapore’s July 11 temperature distribution reflects the prevailing southwest monsoon and a forecast shift toward drier conditions in the second week, which can reduce afternoon cloud cover and allow surface heating to reach 31–32 °C. Official outlooks from the Meteorological Service Singapore indicate daily maxima typically between 33–34 °C early in the month but note that localized thundery showers, common in late morning and afternoon, frequently cap readings near or below 31 °C. A developing El Niño pattern supports modestly above-average warmth and lower rainfall overall, keeping the probability mass concentrated on 30–32 °C while limiting odds for extremes. Updated model runs and daily NEA guidance through the weekend will clarify how much convective activity develops on the 11th.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast runs from major models indicate Milan’s July 11 maximum will likely fall between 30–34 °C, with ensemble spreads reflecting modest uncertainty over daytime cloud cover and boundary-layer mixing across the Po Valley. High-pressure ridging continues to favor clear skies and strong insolation, yet small timing shifts in any afternoon convection or wind could trim or boost the peak by 1–2 °C at official stations such as Malpensa. Traders therefore assign the highest implied probabilities to 30–32 °C outcomes, consistent with the narrow historical range for early-July maxima under similar synoptic patterns and the limited window before final observations are recorded.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Latest numerical weather prediction guidance from ECMWF and GFS ensembles, together with AEMET’s official forecast, places Madrid’s maximum on 11 July near 37–38 °C.**  

Clear skies, strong daytime insolation under a persistent subtropical ridge, and light southerly or calm winds are expected to allow efficient boundary-layer heating, while the city’s urban heat-island effect adds 1–2 °C relative to surrounding rural stations. Ensemble spread remains modest at the 48–72-hour range, supporting the market’s concentration on the 37 °C and 38 °C bins; any late-model shift toward slightly stronger subsidence or reduced cloud cover could push probabilities toward 39 °C, whereas an earlier sea-breeze intrusion or increased low-level moisture would favor the lower side of the distribution. Updated high-resolution runs and the next AEMET bulletin are the next key data points.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent ensemble forecasts from the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts (ECMWF) and Global Forecast System (GFS) cluster Warsaw’s July 11 maximum near 21–23 °C at Chopin Airport, reflecting a transitional pattern after late-June heat. Weak Atlantic low-pressure influence and variable cloud cover are limiting daytime insolation and advecting cooler maritime air, keeping readings below seasonal normals of ~24 °C. Trader consensus centers on 22 °C because small differences in predicted afternoon cloud thickness or boundary-layer mixing can shift the peak by 1–2 °C, while stronger ridging or clearer skies would push probabilities toward 24 °C or higher. Official IMGW-PIB observations will resolve the market.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts from models and the Central Weather Administration point to possible showers, overcast skies, or typhoon-related moisture around July 11, which suppress maximum temperatures below typical July peaks of 32–33 °C in Taipei. These conditions explain why traders assign the highest implied probabilities to 28–30 °C outcomes while giving 32 °C or higher only 17.6 percent. Updated model runs and official briefings in the next 48 hours will likely shift consensus as precipitation timing and intensity become clearer.

eventMetadata.context_description: Forecast uncertainty from numerical weather models, particularly around cloud cover, humidity, and thunderstorm timing on July 11, drives the tight clustering of trader odds between 29°C and 32°C. Beijing’s summer monsoon-influenced pattern often brings scattered convection that reduces solar insolation and caps daily maxima below seasonal averages near 31°C, with recent model consensus showing variable precipitation chances that could shift highs by 2–3°C. Official guidance from agencies like the China Meteorological Administration will refine these details in the next 48 hours, directly impacting resolution as traders weigh urban heat-island effects against potential cooling from rain.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecast guidance for Wuhan places the July 11 maximum near 36–38 °C, aligning with the market’s tight clustering around those bins. Subtropical monsoon conditions, elevated humidity from the Yangtze and numerous urban lakes, and variable cloud cover create narrow uncertainty bands; clear skies under a ridge favor the upper end while scattered convection or stronger winds cap readings. Historical July climatology shows typical highs of 32–35 °C with occasional spikes above 38 °C, but the final 48-hour model runs and local observations will determine whether the peak settles at 36 °C, 37 °C, or 38 °C.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded by NOAA at the Istanbul Airport in degrees Celsius on 11 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from NOAA, specifically the highest reading under the "Temp" column for all times on this day, available here: https://www.weather.gov/wrh/timeseries?site=LTFM

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the "Switch to Metric Units" button until the relevant table displays °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent ensemble forecasts from the ECMWF and Turkish Meteorological Service place Istanbul's July 11 daytime maximum near 27–29 °C under a moderately moist air mass with limited insolation from variable cloud cover and Bosphorus sea-breeze cooling. This consensus underpins the market's leading 28 °C outcome at 32.5 % implied probability, while the broad distribution across 26–30 °C reflects remaining uncertainty in exact convective timing and boundary-layer mixing. Historical July climatology centers near 29 °C, but short-term model updates through the next 48 hours remain the dominant driver of any repricing ahead of official station resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Latest short-range forecasts from the Korea Meteorological Administration and AccuWeather project Busan’s July 11 daytime maximum near 30–31°C amid typical early-July monsoon transition conditions, with sea breezes off the Korea Strait providing some moderation. This consensus, combined with historical July high distributions centered around 28–31°C, underpins the market’s leading implied probabilities for 33°C and 34°C. Persistent warmth and variable cloud cover could push readings a degree or two higher, while increased precipitation or stronger onshore flow would cap outcomes nearer 32°C. Updated KMA model runs and official observations over the next 48 hours remain the key variables traders are monitoring ahead of resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Current short-range forecasts from KNMI and ECMWF ensembles position 26–28°C as the most probable range for Amsterdam’s maximum temperature on July 11, 2026, two days ahead.** Model consensus shows a warm anomaly across northern Europe with southerly flow and clear or partly cloudy conditions allowing daytime heating, but without the strong subsidence or Spanish plume setup needed for 30°C-plus readings. This explains the market’s heavy clustering on 27°C (35.5%), 28°C (24.5%), and 26°C (21.5%), which together capture over 80% of implied probability.

Recent model updates have tightened around these values, with limited spread in the ensembles and no major shifts in steering patterns or cloud cover forecasts that would favor cooler or significantly hotter outcomes. Historical July averages near 22–24°C provide context, yet the current pattern supports a modest positive anomaly consistent with the leading bins. Traders are monitoring the next KNMI and ECMWF runs, plus any afternoon observations on July 10, for refinements before resolution at noon local time on July 11.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus assigns the highest implied probability (29.5%) to a 32°C high in Kuala Lumpur on July 11, with 31°C and 33°C close behind, reflecting short-range forecast uncertainty around the city’s typical July maximum of 31–32°C. Urban heat-island effects in the Klang Valley add slight upward bias, but model spread in shower timing and intensity creates the narrow distribution across adjacent outcomes. Updated MetMalaysia guidance and ensemble runs from ECMWF and GFS over the next 48 hours will likely tighten the outlook before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Current forecast models from sources including the Saudi Meteorological Authority and global ensembles point to a July 11 peak near 37–38 °C under stable subtropical high pressure and moderate northwesterly flow off the Red Sea. Diurnal heating peaks midday with limited cloud cover and sea-surface temperatures near 31 °C moderating extremes while coastal humidity elevates apparent heat. Historical July climatology centers on 37 °C averages with low day-to-day variance absent strong subsidence or Saharan intrusions. The tightly clustered market probabilities around 36–39 °C capture residual model spread in maximum temperature resolution, typical for coastal sites where small steering or moisture shifts can alter the daily high by 1–2 °C before the 11th.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent short-term forecasts from the South African Weather Service and global models indicate mild anticyclonic conditions over the Western Cape, supporting daytime maxima near the July climatological average of 17–18 °C but with modest warm-side bias from clearer skies and light northerly flow. This underpins the tight clustering of market-implied odds around 19–21 °C. Key differentiators include the precise timing of any weak Atlantic trough, which could cap highs at 18 °C or below via increased cloud cover and onshore breeze, versus sustained high pressure that permits 20–21 °C under stronger insolation. Model spread remains modest two days out, yet small shifts in 850 hPa temperatures or boundary-layer mixing will decide resolution among these leading outcomes.

This market will resolve to the temperature range that contains the highest temperature recorded at the Guangzhou Baiyun International Airport Station in degrees Celsius on 11 Jul '26.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Guangzhou Baiyun International Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/cn/guangzhou/ZGGG.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: Recent forecasts point to peak highs near 36–38°C in Guangzhou on July 11 amid typical midsummer subtropical conditions, with model consensus and recent observations supporting the market's tight clustering around 37–38°C. High humidity from the East Asian monsoon, combined with variable cloud cover and potential afternoon thunderstorms, can suppress daytime maxima by limiting solar insolation while also trapping heat overnight; clearer intervals or lighter winds would favor the upper end near 38–39°C, whereas heavier rain or increased convective activity could cap readings at 36°C or below. Historical July averages hover around 32–33°C with occasional spikes above 37°C driven by regional pressure patterns, underscoring the narrow uncertainty window traders are pricing into these closely matched outcomes.

Coastal sea-breeze circulation and elevated humidity from the advancing southwest monsoon are capping daytime heating, while partial cloud cover and light winds introduce modest day-to-day variability. Historical July averages hover around 31–32°C, yet brief clearing or delayed showers could push readings a degree higher. Traders weigh the narrow spread against model uncertainty in exact cloud timing and wind strength, with new guidance expected within 48 hours that may further tighten or widen the implied probabilities.

eventMetadata.context_description:
**Forecast models indicate a mild winter day in São Paulo on July 11, 2026, with daily maximum temperatures most likely ranging from 27–29 °C, aligning closely with current market-implied odds (28 °C at 36.5 %, 27 °C at 27.5 %, 29 °C at 12.5 %).** 

July represents the core of the Southern Hemisphere winter dry season, when average highs typically fall in the low-to-mid 20s °C. Recent cold-front passages earlier in the month delivered showers and brief cooling across the Southeast, but high-pressure dominance has since stabilized conditions, favoring clearer skies and modest daytime warming. Numerical guidance (including INMET and international models) shows limited cloud cover and light northerly flow, supporting temperatures a few degrees above seasonal norms without extreme heat advection. 

Key variables separating the leading outcomes include boundary-layer mixing depth, exact timing of any residual moisture, and urban heat-island effects at official measurement sites. Traders are weighting the narrow spread between 27–29 °C because ensemble runs remain tightly clustered, with only modest differences in predicted solar insolation and wind. Final resolution depends on the highest instantaneous reading from the designated São Paulo station; updated model runs and INMET bulletins over the next 24 hours will provide the clearest signal for any last adjustments in positioning.

eventMetadata.context_description: Trader consensus in the Atlanta July 11 highest-temperature market centers on the latest National Weather Service and ensemble model guidance projecting a daily maximum near 90–91 °F, the bracket holding the highest implied probability at 34.5 %. Recent model runs show modest spread driven by uncertainty in afternoon thunderstorm timing and coverage, which could suppress the peak by a degree or two, alongside the strength of any weak frontal boundary approaching from the northwest. Historical July climatology for Atlanta places the average high at approximately 89 °F, providing context for why lower brackets (88–89 °F at 19.5 %) retain meaningful support while probabilities decay sharply above 93 °F. Key upcoming inputs include the next NWS forecast discussion and updated high-resolution model output expected overnight, which will refine convective and boundary-layer details ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: National Weather Service guidance and ensemble models currently favor a Miami high in the low-to-mid 90s on July 11 under a subtropical ridge with light easterly flow and high humidity. Afternoon sea-breeze convergence often triggers scattered convection that limits peak solar heating, keeping official readings near 90–93 °F and aligning with the market’s leading 92–93 °F (43.5 %) and 90–91 °F (31.5 %) brackets. Saharan dust plumes noted in recent marine forecasts may further suppress storm activity and allow slightly warmer readings, while any earlier or stronger outflow boundaries could cap temperatures in the upper 80s. Updated short-range model runs and the 7 a.m. EDT NWS forecast discussion tomorrow will provide the final data ahead of market resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: San Francisco's marine layer and onshore flow from the Pacific are the dominant controls on July 11 highs, typically capping afternoon temperatures near the 68–70°F seasonal average by limiting solar heating and promoting stratus and fog. Current forecast guidance points to a modest marine push with possible partial clearing, placing the most likely outcomes in the 68–71°F range and explaining why those two bins hold the highest implied probabilities at 25.5% and 26.5%. Model consensus remains tight because July climatology rarely produces extremes in the city proper; small differences in wind direction, inversion strength, or timing of any clearing will decide whether the high settles at 68–69°F or edges into 70–71°F. Traders are weighting the historical base rate heavily while awaiting the final National Weather Service forecast updates before resolution.

eventMetadata.context_description: Mexico City's high-elevation basin location at roughly 2,240 meters, combined with peak rainy-season conditions in July, keeps daytime maxima tightly clustered near the long-term average high of 23–24 °C. Official guidance and ensemble forecasts for July 11 point to partly cloudy skies with scattered afternoon convection and light showers, which can limit peak heating through increased cloud cover, higher humidity, and evaporative cooling. These factors create the narrow spread reflected in trader pricing, where 24 °C, 25 °C, and 26 °C together capture over 70 % of implied probability. Differentiation hinges on the precise timing and intensity of any storms, surface wind patterns, and the official station reading used for market resolution. Updated model runs and morning observations on July 11 will likely refine the outcome before resolution.

The resolution source for this market will be information from Wunderground, specifically the highest temperature recorded for all times on this day for the Marcos A. Gelabert Intl Airport Station, available here: https://www.wunderground.com/history/daily/pa/panama-city/MPMG.

To toggle between Fahrenheit and Celsius, click the gear icon next to the search bar and switch the Temperature setting between °F and °C.

This market can not resolve until the first data point for the following date has been published on the resolution source.

The resolution source for this market measures temperatures to whole degrees Celsius (eg, 9°C). Thus, this is the level of precision that will be used when resolving the market.

Revisions to temperatures recorded within this market's timeframe will be considered until the first datapoint for the following date has been published, after which any alterations will not be considered.

eventMetadata.context_description: In Panama City's tropical wet season, variable afternoon convective cloud cover and rainfall intensity primarily drive uncertainty around the July 11 maximum temperature, with market-implied odds clustered near 30–32°C reflecting typical July averages of 30–31°C. Reduced insolation from widespread thunderstorms or thicker ITCZ-influenced cloud decks caps daytime peaks by limiting surface heating, while breaks in coverage or lighter winds could allow brief rises toward 33°C or higher, though historical data show such extremes remain uncommon. Model consensus on precipitation timing and sea-surface temperatures near the isthmus will shape final outcomes, with official observations resolving the market based on precise daily maxima.
